era_context: The mid-2nd century BCE was marked by the consolidation of Roman power in the Mediterranean following the Third Macedonian War (168 BCE) and the destruction of Carthage (146 BCE). The Han Dynasty in China was in its mature phase under the Xiongnu threat on the northern frontier. The Seleucid Empire was fragmenting under pressure from Parthia and internal dynastic conflict. In India, the Mauryan Empire had long since collapsed, replaced by regional powers including the Satavahana and Indo-Greek kingdoms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse written documentation)
Oceania (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Achaean War and Roman destruction of Corinth
events.description: Following the Achaean League's rebellion against Roman hegemony, Roman general Lucius Mummius defeated the Achaean forces and destroyed the city of Corinth as a demonstration of Roman power. The destruction was total: the city was razed, its population enslaved or killed, and its treasures looted. This event marked the effective end of Greek political independence and the consolidation of Roman control over Greece. The destruction was so complete that Corinth remained largely abandoned for over a century.
events.certainty_note: The destruction of Corinth is well-attested in multiple ancient sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence showing destruction layers dated to 146 BCE. However, the exact year of -150 is incorrect; this event occurred in 146 BCE. Reassigning to correct year.

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaigns against the Xiongnu
events.region: China, Central Asia (Han Empire northern frontier)
events.description: During the reign of Emperor Jing of Han (r. 157–141 BCE), the Han Dynasty conducted sustained military campaigns against Xiongnu incursions on the northern frontier. These campaigns were part of a broader strategy combining military action with diplomatic marriages (heqin) to manage the threat from the nomadic confederation. The period saw significant military expenditure and the development of cavalry tactics to counter Xiongnu mounted warfare.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, chapters on the Han Dynasty and Xiongnu
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Han-Xiongnu conflict in this period is well-established, but specific campaigns in 150 BCE are not precisely dated in surviving sources. Dating is approximate based on reign chronology.
cross_references: Part of broader Han-Xiongnu conflict pattern (-200 to -50)

events.title: Parthian expansion under Mithridates I
events.description: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE) was in the midst of his major expansion campaign, consolidating Parthian control over former Seleucid territories. By 150 BCE, Parthia had already conquered Mesopotamia and was extending influence eastward. This period marked the transformation of Parthia from a minor kingdom into a major imperial power that would eventually rival Rome.
key_figures: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE)
events.certainty_note: The reign of Mithridates I and his territorial expansion are well-documented, but precise dating of specific conquests within his reign is uncertain. The chronology of Parthian expansion is debated among scholars.

events.title: Satavahana Dynasty consolidation in South India
events.description: The Satavahana Dynasty was consolidating its control over the Deccan region of South India during this period. Under rulers like Satakarni I (dates uncertain, possibly mid-2nd century BCE), the Satavahanas were establishing themselves as a major regional power, controlling important trade routes and developing a distinctive culture that blended Vedic and local traditions.

sources.name: Puranas (Hindu religious texts, compiled later but preserving earlier traditions)

events.certainty_note: The chronology of early Satavahana rulers is highly uncertain. Different scholars place Satakarni I anywhere from the 2nd to 1st century BCE. The assignment to 150 BCE is conventional but not firmly established.
cross_references: Part of post-Mauryan South Indian political fragmentation
Contemporary with Indo-Greek kingdoms in North India

disconfirming_evidence: The destruction of Corinth is commonly dated to 146 BCE, not 150 BCE, making the primary event listed for this year anachronistic. Modern scholarship has revised earlier estimates of Parthian expansion under Mithridates I; some scholars now argue the conquest of Mesopotamia occurred later than traditionally believed, possibly in the 140s rather than 150s BCE. The chronology of early Satavahana rulers remains one of the most disputed topics in Indian historiography, with some scholars arguing the dynasty did not emerge until the 1st century BCE, making the assignment of Satakarni I to 150 BCE highly speculative.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 150 BCE is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian sources. Greek and Roman sources dominate, creating a Western-centric bias. Chinese sources (Shiji, Han Shu) provide good documentation of Han Dynasty affairs but are compiled centuries after the events. Indian sources are particularly weak for this period; the Puranas are religious texts compiled much later and contain legendary material. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are essentially absent from the surviving written record. Archaeological evidence is crucial for understanding this period but is unevenly distributed geographically.

graph_edges.note: Achaean War leads directly to destruction of Corinth in 146 BCE
Part of broader Han-Xiongnu conflict spanning centuries
Parthian expansion under Mithridates I eventually leads to Parthian-Roman conflict
